context.sentence: Subsequent cycles were sunitinib for 4 weeks on and 2 weeks off and repeated every 6 weeks in the absence of disease progression or unacceptable toxicity.
The demographic characteristics of the HCC patients enrolled in the study are summarized in Supplementary Table S1 and their hematological toxicities are summarized in Supplementary Table S2.
Unlike for cytotoxic chemotherapeutics, targeted cancer therapies, such as angiogenesis inhibitors, may achieve therapeutic concentrations well below those associated with toxicities.

Several hypotheses may explain the moderate but greater benefit observed in our study, such as: 1) differences in patient HCC baseline characteristics between trials, 2) the absence of hematological toxicities (see Supplementary Table S2), and 3) the inclusion of TACE therapy with doxorubicin.
Sunitinib is a selective tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the angiogenic biomarker: soluble vascular endothelial growth factor receptor‐2 (sVEGFR2).
Moreover, the model quantitatively links the dynamics of an angiogenesis biomarker to TTP and accurately predicts observed literature‐reported results of placebo treatment.

Since HCC is a particularly vascular solid tumor, sunitinib, an antiangiogenic and tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the VGFR, was investigated in advanced HCC patients. • WHAT QUESTION DID THIS STUDY ADDRESS? ☑ We utilized a modeling and simulation approach to identify and qualify a plasma biomarker that links active drug exposure (ADE) of sunitinib to its antitumor activity and time‐to‐tumor progression (TTP) • WHAT THIS STUDY ADDS TO OUR KNOWLEDGE ☑ Our findings suggest that ADE inhibits sVEGFR2, an angiogenesis biomarker, resulting in tumor growth inhibition, and that its daily exposure is a reliable predictor for TTP. • HOW THIS MIGHT CHANGE DRUG DISCOVERY, DEVELOPMENT, AND/OR THERAPEUTICS ☑ Our model constitutes a rational tool to determine an optimal timepoint for the assessment of angiogenesis in HCC patients based on sVEGFR2 dynamics and link it to TTP.

In the present study, an exposure–response modeling framework for sunitinib in HCC was developed, whereby the angiogenesis biomarker dynamics (i.e., sVEGFR2) played a pivotal role in relating the active drug exposure to its antitumor activity and to the time‐to‐tumor progression (TTP).

context.section: Assays for sunitinib, SU12662, and sVEGFR2 plasma concentrations
context.sentence: Plasma concentrations of sVEGFR2 were determined using an enzyme‐linked immunosorbent assay kit, following the manufacturer's instructions (eBioScience, San Diego, CA), with a lower limit of detection of 7 pg/mL.

A reduction in Ktrans was associated with a reduction in tumor vascular permeability, perfusion, and growth following the treatment of mice bearing PC‐3 human prostate adenocacrcinomas with a VEGF receptor‐2 tyrosine kinase inhibitor.28

context.section: PK modeling of sunitinib and SU12662
context.sentence: Briefly, the models each consisted of a two‐compartment model with linear elimination from the central compartment (CLD/Fcentral, CLM/Fcentral) and a first‐order absorption (kaD, kaM) from the gastrointestinal tract.

The effects of available covariates, including body weight, age, gender, and the blood‐to‐tumor permeability parameter Ktrans, were tested for explaining the variability of model parameters controlling the drug and metabolite PK, sVEGFR2 concentrations, and TGI kinetics.
Drug (D) and metabolite (M) were described with two‐compartment models including linear elimination (CLD, CLM) and first‐order absorption from the gastrointestinal tract (kaD, kaM).
For each, the final model included a central, a peripheral, and an absorption compartment.
The absorption was characterized as a first‐order process, and the disposition was captured with a linear clearance.
The clearances and volumes of distribution (central and peripheral) were apparent values, and the final parameter estimates are summarized in Table 1.

Owing to lack of data in the absorption and distribution phases of drug and metabolite, the typical or mean first‐order absorption rate constants, the intercompartmental clearances, and peripheral volumes of distribution were all fixed to values extracted from the literature30 (Table 1).

On the contrary, trough concentrations facilitated the estimation of the remaining parameters (i.e., central volume of distribution and linear disposition) by means of prior information on the estimates and distributions of these parameters.

Following sunitinib treatment, the observed plasma concentrations of sVEGFR2 in HCC patients gradually decreased over time from a mean baseline value of 15.7 µg/L to nearly fall into the range of healthy subjects plasma concentrations 5.5–10 µg/L.35, 36 The proposed model described very well the temporal changes in the dynamics of this biomarker (Figure 2 c), where the simulated median captures the trend in sVEGFR2 concentrations, and the predicted 95% CI covers the majority of the data.
The potency of ACub was estimated at 0.77 (µg/L)−1.
The potency of ΔsVEGFR2 to inhibit HCC growth was ΔIC50 = 1.83 µg/L.
The permeability parameter, Ktrans, was identified as a significant covariate on ΔIC50.

For example, plasma sVEGFR2 has previously been identified as a biomarker of angiogenesis in GIST,41 in which its plasma concentrations were consistently modulated by sunitinib treatment; however, there was no clear correlation with clinical response.

This mechanism was integrated in our model, and previously by others,33, 44, 45 with ACub, derived based on in vitro data15, 16, 17 and kinase inhibition,31 as a driver to the inhibitory effect on biomarker expression.
The estimated drug potency on sVEGFR2 inhibition was 0.77 (µg/L)−1, which is consistent with previous results from modeling sunitinib effects on sVEGFR2 response in healthy subjects.

The fitted ΔsVEGFR2 potency (1.83 µg/L) is comparable to the in vitro measured IC50 of sunitinib for cells expressing VEGFR2 (10 nM or 1.6 µg/L),36, 48 supporting the model in describing HCC growth inhibition kinetics.
No particular temporal trend was observed for the permeability parameter Ktrans.

However, its influence as a covariate on the potency of sVEGFR2 validates its use as a marker for the control of vessel leakiness, and suggests it might represent a determinant of HCC response to sunitinib.
In this analysis, we used sVEGFR2 as a surrogate biomarker to angiogenesis.

nodes.label: PMC5131886
nodes.title: Bridging Sunitinib Exposure to Time‐to‐Tumor Progression in Hepatocellular Carcinoma Patients With Mathematical Modeling of an Angiogenic Biomarker

nodes.label: It is presently approved for the treatment of renal cell carcinoma (RCC) and ima
Slope effect of ACub on VegfR2
The potency of ΔsVEGFR2 to inhibit HCC growth was ΔIC50 = 1.83 µg/L.
Figure 1
nodes.caption: Pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic model for sunitinib and SU12662. Drug (D) and metabolite (M) were described with two‐compartment models including linear elimination (CLD, CLM) and first‐order absorption from the gastrointestinal tract (kaD, kaM). The fraction of drug metabolized into SU12662 was fixed (fM = 0.21). The active unbound concentration, ACub=fub,D.CD+fub,M.CM, with fub,D and fub,M as the free fractions of drug and metabolite, inhibits sVEGFR2 production, which was captured with an indirect response model with a zero‐order production rate constant (kin) and a first‐order removal rate constant (kout). The difference of sVEGFR2 from its baseline concentration (ΔsVEGFR2) inhibits the growth rate constant (kg) controlling HCC tumor progression. The symbols are defined in the Methods.

nodes.label: Figure 2
nodes.caption: Visual predictive check (VPC) plots vs. time for (a) sunitinib plasma concentrations, (b) SU12662 plasma concentrations, (c) sVEGFR2 plasma concentrations, and (d) tumor volume kinetics. Solid circles represent the observed data. The gray area identifies the 5th and 95th percentiles of the predicted data, and dark solid lines represent the 50th percentile (median) of the predicted data. The gray solid line in (d) represents a model simulation of the trajectory of the tumor growth in placebo‐treated patients. The confidence interval includes the majority of the data and the median is centrally located, although the number of observations outside the predicted 90% confidence interval is slightly above the expected 10%. These VPCs show no specific deviation of predicted vs. observed data, which qualifies the model as being structurally sound and supports the veracity of the estimated parameter values and concentrations.

nodes.label: Figure 3
nodes.caption: Model‐predicted TTP probabilities in patients with unresectable HCC. The solid black line represents the observed TTP probability. The solid red lines are the 5th and 95th percentiles of the predicted data, and the red dashed line is the 50th percentile (median) of the predicted data in patients treated with sunitinib. Model simulated TTP probability in placebo‐treated patients is shown in green, where solid lines represent the 5th and 95th percentiles and the dashed line is the 50th percentile (median).

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/parameter/3cc293d705608e3d> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/context_sentence> "Supporting Information\n\nFile: PSP4-5-297-s011.docx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document); parser_hint=document\n\nModel code for the time-to-tumor progression:\n$PROBLEM PK of drug and metabolite, sVEGFR2, TTP\n$MODEL\nCOMP = ( Qa )\nCOMP = ( Qc )\nCOMP = ( Qp )\nCOMP = ( Qam )\nCOMP = ( Qcm )\nCOMP = ( Qpm )\nCOMP = (svgfR2)\nCOMP = (Hazard)\n$PSI Z0 Z1 Z2\n$REG ka1 V1 Q V2 CL ka2 V1m Qm V2m CLm kout alfa R0 TG0 Base AUC TTP\n$PK\n; parameters for parent drug\nk10 = CL/V1\nk12 = Q/V1\nk21 = Q/V2\n; Parameters for active metabolite\nk10m = CLm /V1m\nk12m = Qm /V1m\nk21m = Qm /V2m\n; Intermediate equations needed for sVEGFR2 equation\nkin= R0* kout\nkd = 4\nACub =0.05*(Qc/V1 )+ 0.1*( Qm /V1m)\nINH= ACub /( kd+ACub )\nbeta= 1/(1+alfa*INH)\ndelta =(Base-QvegfR2)\nImax = 1\nINH_TG = 1-(Imax*delta /( IC50+delta))\n; kng derived from Ln(2)/ kng = 114*(TG0)^0.14 see manuscript\nkng = 0.006*(TG0)^-0.14\n; Equations for the hazard, the survival ( i.e TTP), and the probability of TTP\nRisk = Hazard\nSurv = exp ( -Risk)\nProb_Surv = Surv *Risk\nSurv_2=1 /( 1+exp( Surv ))\nProb_Surv_2= exp ( Prob_Surv )/(1+exp( Prob_Surv ))\n$ODE\nQsvgfR2_0 = R0\n; Equations for parent drug\n;;; ;Input dose for parent drug is in nmol\nDDT_Qa = -ka1* Qa\nDDT_Qc = ka2* Qa -k10* Qc - k12*Qc + k21* Qp\nDDT_Qp = k12* Qc - k21* Qp\n; Equations for active metabolite\n;;; ;Input dose for active metabolite is fm *Dose of parent drug in nmol\nDDT_Qam = -ka2* Qam\nDDT_Qcm = ka2* Qam -k10m* Qcm - k12m* Qcm + k21m* Qpm\nDDT_Qpm = k12m* Qcm - k21m* Qpm\n; Equation for sVEGFR2\nDDT_QsvgfR2 = kin*beta- kout *QsvgfR2\n; Equation for the hazard function\nDDT_Hazard = Z0* exp ( Z1*TTP+Z2*AUC)\n$CATEGORICAL (0 ,2 )\nLOGIT1(Y<=0 )= Surv_2\nLOGIT1(Y<=1 )= Prob_Surv_2\n$OUTPUT\nOUTPUT1 = LL1" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/parameter/a103356c787608d3> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/context_sentence> "Supporting Information\n\nFile: PSP4-5-297-s012.docx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document); parser_hint=document\n\nModel code for PK of drug and metabolite and sVEGFR2 and tumor growth inhibition\n$PROBLEM PK of drug and metabolite, sVEGFR2, TGI\n$MODEL\nCOMP = ( Qa )\nCOMP = ( Qc )\nCOMP = ( Qp )\nCOMP = ( Qam )\nCOMP = ( Qcm )\nCOMP = ( Qpm )\nCOMP = (svgfR2)\nCOMP = (TG)\n$PSI IC50\n$REG ka1 V1 Q V2 CL ka2 V1m Qm V2m CLm kout alfa R0 TG0 Base\n$PK\n; parameters for parent drug\nk10 = CL/V1\nk12 = Q/V1\nk21 = Q/V2\n; Parameters for active metabolite\nk10m = CLm /V1m\nk12m = Qm /V1m\nk21m = Qm /V2m\n; Intermediate equations needed for sVEGFR2 equation\nkin= R0* kout\nkd = 4\nACub =0.05*(Qc/V1 )+ 0.1*( Qm /V1m)\nINH= ACub /( kd+ACub )\nbeta= 1/(1+alfa*INH)\ndelta =(Base-QvegfR2)\nImax = 1\nINH_TG = 1-(Imax*delta /( IC50+delta))\n; kng derived from Ln(2)/ kng = 114*(TG0)^0.14 see manuscript\nkng = 0.006*(TG0)^-0.14\n$ODE\nQ sv gfR2_0 = R0\n; Equations for parent drug\n;;; ;Input dose for parent drug is in nmol\nDDT_Qa = -ka1* Qa\nDDT_Qc = ka2* Qa -k10* Qc - k12*Qc + k21* Qp\nDDT_Qp = k12* Qc - k21* Qp\n; Equations for active metabolite\n;;; ;Input dose for active metabolite is fm *Dose of parent drug in nmol\nDDT_Qam = -ka2* Qam\nDDT_Qcm = ka2* Qam -k10m* Qcm - k12m* Qcm + k21m* Qpm\nDDT_Qpm = k12m* Qcm - k21m* Qpm\n; Equation for sVEGFR2\nDDT_QsvgfR2 = kin*beta- kout *QvegfR2\n; Equation for tumor growth inhibition\nDDT_TG = kng *INH_TG*TG\n$OUTPUT\nOUTPUT1 = TG" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/cfe4c3ac8d401b24> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/evidence_quote> "Supporting Information\n\nFile: PSP4-5-297-s012.docx (application/vnd.openxmlformats-officedocument.wordprocessingml.document); parser_hint=document\n\nModel code for PK of drug and metabolite and sVEGFR2 and tumor growth inhibition\n$PROBLEM PK of drug and metabolite, sVEGFR2, TGI\n$MODEL\nCOMP = ( Qa )\nCOMP = ( Qc )\nCOMP = ( Qp )\nCOMP = ( Qam )\nCOMP = ( Qcm )\nCOMP = ( Qpm )\nCOMP = (svgfR2)\nCOMP = (TG)\n$PSI IC50\n$REG ka1 V1 Q V2 CL ka2 V1m Qm V2m CLm kout alfa R0 TG0 Base\n$PK\n; parameters for parent drug\nk10 = CL/V1\nk12 = Q/V1\nk21 = Q/V2\n; Parameters for active metabolite\nk10m = CLm /V1m\nk12m = Qm /V1m\nk21m = Qm /V2m\n; Intermediate equations needed for sVEGFR2 equation\nkin= R0* kout\nkd = 4\nACub =0.05*(Qc/V1 )+ 0.1*( Qm /V1m)\nINH= ACub /( kd+ACub )\nbeta= 1/(1+alfa*INH)\ndelta =(Base-QvegfR2)\nImax = 1\nINH_TG = 1-(Imax*delta /( IC50+delta))\n; kng derived from Ln(2)/ kng = 114*(TG0)^0.14 see manuscript\nkng = 0.006*(TG0)^-0.14\n$ODE\nQ sv gfR2_0 = R0\n; Equations for parent drug\n;;; ;Input dose for parent drug is in nmol\nDDT_Qa = -ka1* Qa\nDDT_Qc = ka2* Qa -k10* Qc - k12*Qc + k21* Qp\nDDT_Qp = k12* Qc - k21* Qp\n; Equations for active metabolite\n;;; ;Input dose for active metabolite is fm *Dose of parent drug in nmol\nDDT_Qam = -ka2* Qam\nDDT_Qcm = ka2* Qam -k10m* Qcm - k12m* Qcm + k21m* Qpm\nDDT_Qpm = k12m* Qcm - k21m* Qpm\n; Equation for sVEGFR2\nDDT_QsvgfR2 = kin*beta- kout *QvegfR2\n; Equation for tumor growth inhibition\nDDT_TG = kng *INH_TG*TG\n$OUTPUT\nOUTPUT1 = TG" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/mechanism_step/0d214a1d695f119e> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/evidence_quote> "Since HCC is a particularly vascular solid tumor, sunitinib, an antiangiogenic and tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the VGFR, was investigated in advanced HCC patients. • WHAT QUESTION DID THIS STUDY ADDRESS? ☑ We utilized a modeling and simulation approach to identify and qualify a plasma biomarker that links active drug exposure (ADE) of sunitinib to its antitumor activity and time‐to‐tumor progression (TTP) • WHAT THIS STUDY ADDS TO OUR KNOWLEDGE ☑ Our findings suggest that ADE inhibits sVEGFR2, an angiogenesis biomarker, resulting in tumor growth inhibition, and that its daily exposure is a reliable predictor for TTP. • HOW THIS MIGHT CHANGE DRUG DISCOVERY, DEVELOPMENT, AND/OR THERAPEUTICS ☑ Our model constitutes a rational tool to determine an optimal timepoint for the assessment of angiogenesis in HCC patients based on sVEGFR2 dynamics and link it to TTP." <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .
<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/mechanism_step/0d214a1d695f119e> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/subject_label> "ADE" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .
<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/mechanism_step/0d214a1d695f119e> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/object_label> "sVEGFR2, an angiogenesis biomarker, resulting in tumor growth" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/239c30236dfc278d> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/evidence_quote> "Since HCC is a particularly vascular solid tumor, sunitinib, an antiangiogenic and tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the VGFR, was investigated in advanced HCC patients. • WHAT QUESTION DID THIS STUDY ADDRESS? ☑ We utilized a modeling and simulation approach to identify and qualify a plasma biomarker that links active drug exposure (ADE) of sunitinib to its antitumor activity and time‐to‐tumor progression (TTP) • WHAT THIS STUDY ADDS TO OUR KNOWLEDGE ☑ Our findings suggest that ADE inhibits sVEGFR2, an angiogenesis biomarker, resulting in tumor growth inhibition, and that its daily exposure is a reliable predictor for TTP. • HOW THIS MIGHT CHANGE DRUG DISCOVERY, DEVELOPMENT, AND/OR THERAPEUTICS ☑ Our model constitutes a rational tool to determine an optimal timepoint for the assessment of angiogenesis in HCC patients based on sVEGFR2 dynamics and link it to TTP." <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/1f0f115d9c47b9db> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/chunk_id> "chunk_002" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .
<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/1f0f115d9c47b9db> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/evidence_quote> "Since HCC is a particularly vascular solid tumor, sunitinib, an antiangiogenic and tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the VGFR, was investigated in advanced HCC patients. • WHAT QUESTION DID THIS STUDY ADDRESS? ☑ We utilized a modeling and simulation approach to identify and qualify a plasma biomarker that links active drug exposure (ADE) of sunitinib to its antitumor activity and time‐to‐tumor progression (TTP) • WHAT THIS STUDY ADDS TO OUR KNOWLEDGE ☑ Our findings suggest that ADE inhibits sVEGFR2, an angiogenesis biomarker, resulting in tumor growth inhibition, and that its daily exposure is a reliable predictor for TTP. • HOW THIS MIGHT CHANGE DRUG DISCOVERY, DEVELOPMENT, AND/OR THERAPEUTICS ☑ Our model constitutes a rational tool to determine an optimal timepoint for the assessment of angiogenesis in HCC patients based on sVEGFR2 dynamics and link it to TTP." <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/fc26843809809c7e> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/evidence_quote> "Following sunitinib treatment, the observed plasma concentrations of sVEGFR2 in HCC patients gradually decreased over time from a mean baseline value of 15.7 µg/L to nearly fall into the range of healthy subjects plasma concentrations 5.5–10 µg/L.35, 36 The proposed model described very well the temporal changes in the dynamics of this biomarker (Figure 2 c), where the simulated median captures the trend in sVEGFR2 concentrations, and the predicted 95% CI covers the majority of the data." <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/figure_annotation/64f6a1d7ece8354b> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/figure_label> "Figure 1" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .
<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/figure_annotation/64f6a1d7ece8354b> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/caption> "Pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic model for sunitinib and SU12662. Drug (D) and metabolite (M) were described with two‐compartment models including linear elimination (CLD, CLM) and first‐order absorption from the gastrointestinal tract (kaD, kaM). The fraction of drug metabolized into SU12662 was fixed (fM = 0.21). The active unbound concentration, ACub=fub,D.CD+fub,M.CM, with fub,D and fub,M as the free fractions of drug and metabolite, inhibits sVEGFR2 production, which was captured with an indirect response model with a zero‐order production rate constant (kin) and a first‐order removal rate constant (kout). The difference of sVEGFR2 from its baseline concentration (ΔsVEGFR2) inhibits the growth rate constant (kg) controlling HCC tumor progression. The symbols are defined in the Methods." <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/51e0dde5e0449887> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/chunk_id> "figure_001" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .
<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/51e0dde5e0449887> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/evidence_quote> "Pharmacokinetic/pharmacodynamic model for sunitinib and SU12662. Drug (D) and metabolite (M) were described with two‐compartment models including linear elimination (CLD, CLM) and first‐order absorption from the gastrointestinal tract (kaD, kaM). The fraction of drug metabolized into SU12662 was fixed (fM = 0.21). The active unbound concentration, ACub=fub,D.CD+fub,M.CM, with fub,D and fub,M as the free fractions of drug and metabolite, inhibits sVEGFR2 production, which was captured with an indirect response model with a zero‐order production rate constant (kin) and a first‐order removal rate constant (kout). The difference of sVEGFR2 from its baseline concentration (ΔsVEGFR2) inhibits the growth rate constant (kg) controlling HCC tumor progression. The symbols are defined in the Methods." <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/figure_annotation/6846e8db6b69560d> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/caption> "Visual predictive check (VPC) plots vs. time for (a) sunitinib plasma concentrations, (b) SU12662 plasma concentrations, (c) sVEGFR2 plasma concentrations, and (d) tumor volume kinetics. Solid circles represent the observed data. The gray area identifies the 5th and 95th percentiles of the predicted data, and dark solid lines represent the 50th percentile (median) of the predicted data. The gray solid line in (d) represents a model simulation of the trajectory of the tumor growth in placebo‐treated patients. The confidence interval includes the majority of the data and the median is centrally located, although the number of observations outside the predicted 90% confidence interval is slightly above the expected 10%. These VPCs show no specific deviation of predicted vs. observed data, which qualifies the model as being structurally sound and supports the veracity of the estimated parameter values and concentrations." <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/8a0883e611539ce2> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/chunk_id> "chunk_002" <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .
<https://takahuman.ai/kg/node/evidence/8a0883e611539ce2> <https://takahuman.ai/kg/prop/evidence_quote> "Since HCC is a particularly vascular solid tumor, sunitinib, an antiangiogenic and tyrosine kinase inhibitor of the VGFR, was investigated in advanced HCC patients. • WHAT QUESTION DID THIS STUDY ADDRESS? ☑ We utilized a modeling and simulation approach to identify and qualify a plasma biomarker that links active drug exposure (ADE) of sunitinib to its antitumor activity and time‐to‐tumor progression (TTP) • WHAT THIS STUDY ADDS TO OUR KNOWLEDGE ☑ Our findings suggest that ADE inhibits sVEGFR2, an angiogenesis biomarker, resulting in tumor growth inhibition, and that its daily exposure is a reliable predictor for TTP. • HOW THIS MIGHT CHANGE DRUG DISCOVERY, DEVELOPMENT, AND/OR THERAPEUTICS ☑ Our model constitutes a rational tool to determine an optimal timepoint for the assessment of angiogenesis in HCC patients based on sVEGFR2 dynamics and link it to TTP." <https://takahuman.ai/kg/graph/PMC5131886> .

summary.title: Bridging Sunitinib Exposure to Time‐to‐Tumor Progression in Hepatocellular Carcinoma Patients With Mathematical Modeling of an Angiogenic Biomarker

relations.object_label: VEGFRs, PDGFRs, and other growth factors

qualifiers.quote: This metabolite was shown to be equipotent to the parent drug in biochemical tyrosine kinase and cellular proliferation assays towards VEGFR.16, 17 Sunitinib selectively binds and inhibits VEGFRs, PDGFRs, and other growth factors.

relations.object_label: VEGFRs, PDGFRs, and other growth factors
the growth rate constant (kg) controlling HCC tumor progression

qualifiers.quote: The difference of sVEGFR2 from its baseline concentration (ΔsVEGFR2) inhibits the growth rate constant (kg) controlling HCC tumor progression.

relations.object_label: the growth rate constant (kg) controlling HCC tumor progression

relations.subject_label: free fractions of drug and metabolite
free fractions of drug and metabolite

qualifiers.quote: The active unbound concentration, ACub=fub,D.CD+fub,M.CM, with fub,D and fub,M as the free fractions of drug and metabolite, inhibits sVEGFR2 production, which was captured with an indirect response model with a zero‐order production rate constant (kin) and a first‐order removal rate constant (kout).

The results suggest that 1) active drug concentration (i.e., sunitinib and its metabolite) inhibits the release of sVEGFR2 and that such inhibition is associated with TGI, and 2) daily sVEGFR2 exposure is likely a reliable predictor for the TTP in HCC patients.
